variations.name: Main Line

annotation.moveRationale: Bxd2+ exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Nc6 develops toward the center, supporting the pawn structure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Qe7 activates the queen, increasing pressure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Be7 develops the bishop to a modest but solid square, preparing to castle in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
c5 challenges the center from the flank in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Bb7 develops a piece to an active square in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Bg2 develops a piece to an active square in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
b6 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
g3 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
a5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Bd2 develops a piece to an active square in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Nbd2 develops a piece to an active square in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Nfd2 develops a piece to an active square in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
b5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
d5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
c5 challenges the center from the flank in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Nc3 develops toward the center, supporting the pawn structure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Bb4+ delivers check, seizing the initiative in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Nf3 develops a piece and contributes to White's fight for the center of the board. This move strengthens the d4 and e5 squares and brings White a move closer to castling kingside.
e6 is a move with several purposes. First, it opens a diagonal for the development of Black's dark-squared bishop.
White takes more space and increases the control of the important d5 square. This move order allows White's queenside knight to develop to the active c3 square without blocking the c-pawn.
Black controls e4 while developing a knight. This is the most flexible response to 1.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.

Bxd2+ exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Bogo-Indian Defense.
Bd2 develops a piece to an active square in the Bogo-Indian Defense.

Bb7 develops a piece to an active square in the Bogo-Indian Defense.

With this move, White decides to challenge the h1-a8 diagonal immediately and fianchetto his own light-squared bishop to counter black's. Black can now play:

*4.

The Queen's Indian Defence is a popular and fairly modern opening whereby black quickly develops his queenside bishop, either by fianchettoing it on b7 to contest the long diagonal, or on a6 to put pressure on white's c-pawn. It has been employed regularly by a number of top players including former world champions like  Anatoly Karpov and Viswanathan Anand.
Nf3 develops a piece and contributes to White's fight for the center of the board. This move strengthens the d4 and e5 squares and brings White a move closer to castling kingside.
e6 is a move with several purposes. First, it opens a diagonal for the development of Black's dark-squared bishop.
White takes more space and increases the control of the important d5 square. This move order allows White's queenside knight to develop to the active c3 square without blocking the c-pawn.
Black controls e4 while developing a knight. This is the most flexible response to 1.
d4 is the Queen's pawn opening. d4 White looks to take over control of the centre.

This is the Catalan Opening, named after the Spanish region of Catalonia, when Grandmaster Savielly Tartakower was asked, at the Barcelona tournament of 1929, to invent an opening system in honor of the region's chess history. Usually, the game continues 3.
